excerpt: Intellect is the name of an infinite potential... Reasoning is a finite force that moves from one subject to another
source_location_hint: Khutba on the nature of intellect and reason

excerpt: You will fight for the interpretation of the Qur'an as I have fought for its revelation
source_location_hint: Sermon at Battle of Jamal

excerpt: He is praised beyond what speakers can express; His grace surpasses enumeration
source_location_hint: Sermon 91 or related theological discourse

excerpt: The plurality of all signs of the universe and the soul return to one truth which is one
source_location_hint: Commentary on Qur'an 41:53

excerpt: If I wish, I can tell you seventy interpretations of each verse of the Qur'an
source_location_hint: Hadith on tafsir and ta'wil

excerpt: The intellect is the inner messenger through which divine messages can be empirically confirmed
source_location_hint: On the relationship between intellect and revelation

excerpt: The spirit between intellect and revelation is boundless in vastness; it receives revelation's messages and conveys them to intellect
source_location_hint: On the hierarchy of intellect, spirit, and soul

excerpt: One of the purposes of sending prophets was to extract the hidden treasures of intellect; revelation awakens and enlivens intellect
source_location_hint: Discourse on knowledge and prophetic mission

excerpt: Allah is praiseworthy yet cannot be praised; His blessings are present yet uncounted; His obligation is binding yet impossible to fulfill
source_location_hint: Theological discourse on divine attributes

excerpt: God has not begotten a son nor is there any god beside Him; if there were, each would have taken what he created
source_location_hint: Quranic verse on divine uniqueness

excerpt: The remembrance of Allah is the noblest worship; one cannot attain true remembrance unless the soul loses itself in remembrance of Allah
source_location_hint: On dhikr and fana billah

excerpt: O Kumayl, these hearts are vessels; the finest preserve their contents... People are of three kinds: the godly scholar, the seeker on the path of salvation, and the heedless masses...
source_location_hint: Complete discourse with Kumayl ibn Ziyad al-Nakha'i

excerpt: Knowledge thrusts them upon the realities of insight; knowledge must be awakened from its latent state in intellect's essence
source_location_hint: On hajama and basirat in the Kumayl sermon

excerpt: But on their hearts is rust which they have earned; the soul's evils cloud intellect like rust upon the heart
source_location_hint: On ran (rust) as moral and spiritual corruption

excerpt: Nor does he speak of his own desire; it is only inspiration that is inspired
source_location_hint: On the Prophet's freedom from personal desire

excerpt: Have you seen him who has taken his desire as his god? Allah has led him astray and sealed his hearing and heart
source_location_hint: On hidden polytheism and worship of desire

excerpt: من عرف نفسہ فقد عرف ربہ
source_location_hint: Cited in Ismaili theological literature and Sufi works

excerpt: Allah is beautiful and loves beautiful things
source_work: Sunan Ibn Mājah and other hadith collections
source_location_hint: Commonly cited in Islamic ethical texts

excerpt: اول العقل التَوَدُّد
source_location_hint: Cited in Ismaili philosophical texts

excerpt: Know that even the slightest hypocrisy is shirk
source_location_hint: Common in devotional teachings

excerpt: My eyes sleep, but my heart remains vigilant
source_location_hint: Cited in Sufi and Islamic teachings

excerpt: Death is as dear to me as a mother's breast to a newborn child
source_location_hint: Aphorisms concerning asceticism and spiritual reality

excerpt: In my sight, your world is as vile as a pig's bone gnawed in the grasp of a leper...The world is like a serpent—gentle to the touch, yet full of deadly venom...To me, this world is as the moisture that flows from a goat's nose
source_location_hint: Sermons and aphorisms on asceticism

excerpt: کان یعظمہ فی عینی، صغار الدنیا فی عینہ
source_location_hint: Praise of believers and ascetic virtue

excerpt: لو کشف الغطاء ما الزدت یقینا
source_location_hint: On certainty and spiritual conviction

excerpt: I bear testimony that there is no god save Allāh. The sincerity of this my testimony has been tested, and its essence—its مُعتقدٌ مصاصُہُ very marrow—is mingled with conviction
source_location_hint: On the essence of sincere testimony

excerpt: Justice is the act of placing things in their proper station
source_work: Letter to Mālik al-Ashtar / Nahj al-Balāgha
source_location_hint: Governance and ethical principles

excerpt: Gabriel came in human form and asked: Tell me of islām (submission)
source_work: Sunan al-Tirmidhī and other hadith collections
source_authority: Hadith tradition; variant: Forty Hadith of Imām al-Nawawī
source_location_hint: The hadith of Gabriel (Jibrīl) on īmān, islām, and ihsān

excerpt: It is to worship Allāh as though you see Him with your own eyes; and if you cannot achieve this, then at least worship Him knowing that He sees you
source_location_hint: Definition of ihsān (excellence) in worship

excerpt: Worship Allāh as though you see Him
source_location_hint: On consciousness of Divine presence

excerpt: Know that every deed depends upon your niyyah (intention)—upon how you regard 'excellence'
source_location_hint: On the role of intention in deeds

excerpt: Pretense, arrogance, and self-laudation are Satan's finest snares, by which he destroys those of excellence
source_location_hint: On spiritual pitfalls and the destruction of ego

excerpt: The ruler who becomes arrogant usurps one of the great attributes of Allah, considering himself entitled to an attribute worthy of Allah alone.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_location_hint: Letter to Malik al-Ashtar on justice and governance

excerpt: Iblis was the first to confront Allah in the field of arrogance, and thus became the source and leader of all arrogance that followed.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_location_hint: Sermon on the Fall of Iblis

excerpt: Shirk is hidden in my Community like the crawling of a black ant upon a black stone in a black night.
source_author: Prophet Muhammad (upon him be peace and blessings)
source_location_hint: on concealed shirk and hypocrisy

excerpt: The wrongdoer can be both a Muslim in profession and a denier of truth in his concealment of the truth from himself and his deeds.
source_location_hint: on the nature of wrongdoing and self-deception

excerpt: The commanding soul (nafs al-ammara) dominates over body, emotion, imagination, intellect, intention, passion, and consciousness.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_location_hint: advice to Malik al-Ashtar on the struggle against the lower self

excerpt: The Imams of Truth are obligated to live their worldly lives at the level of the poorest among their people, so that they might experience the poverty of the poor.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_location_hint: on the lifestyle of just rulers

excerpt: I shall inflict upon you a punishment that will diminish your substance, fracture your spine, and obliterate your reputation.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_location_hint: stern warning against misappropriation of public funds

excerpt: there is a group that worships Allah out of the desire for Paradise and this is commercial worship, one group worships out of fear of Hell and this is slave worship, and a group worships Allah out of gratitude, and this is the worship of the free
source_location_hint: Sermon 176, on types of worship

excerpt: An action becomes acceptable when neither praise nor blame from any creature affects it, and it is neither weakened by external difficulty nor strengthened by internal consequence
source_location_hint: Supplication 47, on sincerity of intention

excerpt: the just person is strengthened by the divine attribute of justice itself and does not despair when results are delayed
source_location_hint: Discussion of divine justice and human virtue

excerpt: My servant does not draw near to Me through anything more beloved to Me than the obligatory duties. My servant continues to draw near to Me through supererogatory acts until I love him. When I love him, I am his hearing by which he hears, his sight by which he sees, his hand by which he grasps, and his feet by which he walks
source_location_hint: Discussion of nearness to God through worship

excerpt: Indeed, God has made remembrance for hearts, through which they hear when deaf and see when blind
source_location_hint: Sermon on the significance of dhikr and remembrance

excerpt: He cannot be seen with the eyes of flesh, but the heart sees him with the truth of faith
source_location_hint: Discussion of knowledge of God through the heart

excerpt: Ali is our master in the knowledge of principles and foundations
source_location_hint: introduction or biographical section on Ali ibn Abi Talib

excerpt: The word dhikr carries extraordinary eloquence in meaning; it encompasses remembrance, reflection, petition, repetition, glorification, and recitation; God Himself has designated the Qur'an as dhikr
source_location_hint: foundational usage of dhikr terminology

excerpt: For the Sufis and saints, zikr becomes a methodical system through which the hearts absorb what the intellect comprehends, transforming the abstract imaginative object into the concrete existence of spiritual realities
source_location_hint: discussions of zikr practice and spiritual development

excerpt: My servant continues drawing near to Me through supererogatory works until I love him; and when I love him, I become his hearing with which he hears, his sight with which he sees, his hand with which he grasps, and his feet with which he walks
source_location_hint: divine love and proximity hadith

excerpt: The first of religion is its knowledge; the word 'first' denotes priority—the foundation, the beginning; the meaning of knowledge encompasses not merely the acquisition of new understanding but the recognition of what already is
source_author: Amir al-Mu'minin Ali ibn Abi Talib
source_location_hint: opening sermon on knowledge and religion

excerpt: Names that are not created by man, but which Allah Himself has chosen for Himself
source_location_hint: Discussion of divine attributes and nomenclature

excerpt: lahija—to be devoted, to repeat, to cleave to; from the saying 'whosoever loves something mentions it with passion'
source_location_hint: On the meaning of constancy in remembrance

excerpt: Ya man asmahu dawa wa dhikruhu shifa—O Thou whose Name is a remedy and whose remembrance is healing
source_location_hint: On the therapeutic power of divine Names

excerpt: Whoever mentions Allah revives his own heart and illuminates his inmost substance (albab, the seat of intellect)
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiled sayings of Imam Ali)
source_location_hint: On the effects of remembrance on the soul

excerpt: Every action has a limit which, once attained, ceases; except the remembrance of Allah—for it knows no ceiling, no terminus
source_location_hint: On the infinite nature of dhikr

excerpt: the state of self-consciousness is created — a state of al-fanāʾ fi-l-dhikr (annihilation in remembrance)
source_location_hint: teachings on spiritual states and self-annihilation in remembrance

excerpt: the heart, which is the mirror of spiritual reality and the locus wherein all moral and spiritual virtues are born
source_location_hint: mystical teachings on the heart as locus of virtue

excerpt: the reality of dhikr becomes one with divine truth itself — at such a moment it may be said inwardly that it is Allah Himself who remembers through the tongue of humanity
source_location_hint: doctrine of divine remembrance and human annihilation

excerpt: when they arrive, they become unified with Him in such manner that no distinction separates them from their Beloved — a spiritual wine reserved for the saints
source_location_hint: mystical stages and divine union

excerpt: the madhkūr (the One remembered) is first, and the dhākir (the rememberer) is second — awwalan min al-madhkūr wa-thānīan min al-dhākir
source_location_hint: doctrine of divine primacy in remembrance

excerpt: the name of Allah is separate from His essence; everything upon which His name appears is a creation — there are ninety-nine names of God, and were name and essence identical, each would constitute a separate god
source_location_hint: doctrine of divine names and attributes

excerpt: I am the Sign of Allah Almighty — I am the mirror of Allah, His pen and His tablet — I am he who possesses the mysteries of the Messenger of Allah
source_authority: authoritative collection of Imam Ali's sermons
source_location_hint: sermon describing divine signs and cosmic mysteries

excerpt: refusal to stop short at the superficial surface of things, be it religious, ethics, politics, manifested an unflinching fidelity to the sacred substance of the religion
source_location_hint: First sermon

excerpt: Ali will fight for the taweel of the Quran as I fought for its tanzil
source_location_hint: Prophetic utterance

excerpt: O Commander of the Faithful! Do you say that Allah is one? Leave him for surely what the bedouin wishes is what we wish for the people
source_location_hint: Account of Battle of Camel

excerpt: that which has no second does not enter into the category of numbers
source_location_hint: Discourse on divine unity

excerpt: He is the First and the Last, the Outward and the Inward
source_location_hint: Al-Hadid (The Iron)

excerpt: We will show them Our Signs in the universe, and in their ownselves, until it becomes manifest to them that this is the truth
source_location_hint: Al-Fussilat (Explained in Detail)

excerpt: Quran consists of a book inscribed between two covers; it speaks not with a tongue, it cannot do without an interpreter
source_location_hint: Sayings of Ali on Quran interpretation

excerpt: The head of a man is the interpreter of his intellect
source_location_hint: Sayings of Ali on intellect

excerpt: the spirit animating the intellect is not restricted by human limitations. It is this spirit which perceives and grasps the divine quality of revelation
source_location_hint: Intellectual-spiritual harmony doctrine

excerpt: to unearth for them the buried treasures of the intellects
source_location_hint: First sermon

excerpt: Eyes see Him not through sight's observation, but hearts see Him through the true principles and beliefs of faith
source_location_hint: Discourse on witnessing the divine

excerpt: laysa li-sifatihee Haddun Mahdud—the divine attributes cannot be confined to a definition
source_location_hint: First sermon opening

excerpt: My Lord is not described in terms of dimension (buxd), nor motion (haraka), nor rest (sukun), nor through coming or going. He is subtle in subtlety but not described by subtlety. He is magnificent in magnificence but not described by magnificence. He is great in greatness but not described by greatness
source_location_hint: Sermon on divine attributes

excerpt: Glorified be Allah above all that they attribute to Him
source_location_hint: Al-Mu'minun (The Believers)

excerpt: the remembrance of Allah constitutes the most excellent form of worship
source_location_hint: Sayings on Dhikr Allah

excerpt: Who made everything He has created good, and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his offspring from semen of worthless water. Then He fashioned him in due proportion, and breathed into him the soul, and He gave you hearing, sight and hearts
source_location_hint: As-Sajdah (The Prostration)

excerpt: Then Allah (may He be glorified), gathered up a quantity of dust...Then He breathed into it of His spirit, and it stood up as a human being, endowed with intellectual powers with which to reflect
source_location_hint: First sermon, creation narrative

excerpt: O you who believe! Answer Allah and His messenger when he calls you to that which will give you life, and know that Allah comes in between a person and his heart
source_location_hint: Al-Anfal (The Spoils)

excerpt: to remind them of the buried treasures of the intellect
source_location_hint: First sermon, on divine call to humanity

excerpt: Those who are the lordly knower, fewest in number yet greatest in sight of Allah, through whom His religion is sustained
source_work: Sermon to Kumayl (Waṣīyyat al-Qada) from Nahj al-Balāgha
source_authority: Ahl al-Bayt scholarly tradition, Ismaili sources
source_location_hint: Nahj al-Balāgha, Sermon 110 / Kirmani commentaries

excerpt: Through the true knowers (al-ʿārifūn), religion remains true to itself and truly manifests in the world
source_location_hint: Kirmani's commentary on sacred knowledge and spiritual realization

excerpt: The shift of consciousness required for knowledge to penetrate into reality is indicated by the Imam pointing to his breast
source_location_hint: Classic commentaries on Kumayl sermon

excerpt: He who understands knows the depth of knowledge - an indication of the buried treasures within true knowledge
source_location_hint: Transmitted in Ismaili and Twelver sources, including Kirmani exegesis

excerpt: The true intellectual (ʿāqil) is engaged in constant struggle against the lower soul, its whims and egoistic tendencies
source_location_hint: Multiple sermons and aphorisms in Nahj al-Balāgha, particularly Sermon 176

excerpt: Hidden idolatry (shirk al-khafī) is more concealed than a black ant on black stone in moonless night
source_work: Hadith qudsi transmitted by Imam ʿAlī
source_authority: Authentic hadith tradition, cited in Ismaili texts
source_location_hint: Kirmani and Ismaili sources; also found in Sunnī hadith collections

excerpt: Pure sincerity (ikhlāṣ) involves active effort to act and think purely for Allah with selflessness
source_work: Sayings and teachings of Imam ʿAlī
source_location_hint: Reflected throughout Nahj al-Balāgha, especially regarding intention and sincerity

excerpt: Prayer in its widest sense includes all means of orientation and attunement to the Real - formal worship, supplication, meditation, remembrance, and contemplation
source_work: Teachings on devotion and spiritual practice
source_author: Imam ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib and Ismaili tradition
source_authority: Ismaili spiritual metaphysics (e.g., Kirmani, Nasir-i Khusraw)
source_location_hint: Nasir-i Khusraw's Wajh-i Din and Jamiʿ al-Hikmatayn; Kirmani's works

excerpt: There is no religion for one who has no intellect - the intellect is foundational to authentic religious practice
source_location_hint: Collected in various hadith works and Ismaili sources, including Kirmani commentaries

excerpt: Sleeping with certainty is better than praying with doubt - prioritizing spiritual certitude over mere outer action
source_work: Sayings of Imam ʿAlī in context of Khārijite excessive ritual
source_authority: Ahl al-Bayt tradition and Shīʿī sources
source_location_hint: Transmitted in Ismaili and Twelver hadith collections
